_notes.structure: Same engine as Spanish version. 16 stage1 + 8 tiebreaker + 24 stage2.
_notes.unified: Single template per item. Age band only changes contextual examples via {6-11: ... | 12-17: ...} placeholder. Resolved by engine.
_notes.placeholders: Gender: {he/she}, {his/her}, {him/her}, {himself/herself} (capitalized at sentence start: {He/She}, {His/Her}). Age: {6-11: kid example | 12-17: teen example}. Resolved by src/lib/childPersonalize.js.
_notes.name_injection: Engine injects child's name in COL1, SAN1, MEL1, FLE1 (one item per Stage 1 temperament block).

COL.template: {6-11: When playing with other children, {he/she} tends to be the one who decides what to play and assigns roles, rather than following what others suggest. | 12-17: When {his/her} group can't decide what to do or where to go, {he/she} is usually the one who cuts through the indecision and proposes a plan.}
When something (a game, a task, a plan) is going slowly because others are hesitating, {he/she} becomes visibly impatient: sighs, hurries others along, or tries to take charge.

When something makes {him/her} angry or frustrated ({6-11: losing a game, something going wrong | 12-17: a bad outcome, an argument}), it shows immediately on {his/her} face, tone, or gestures, even before {he/she} says a word.

When {he/she} disagrees with a rule or decision ({6-11: at home, in class | 12-17: at home, with teachers, with friends}), {he/she} addresses it directly and argues the point — even if it creates tension — rather than staying quiet or giving in to avoid conflict.

SAN.template: When arriving somewhere new or joining a group where {he/she} knows almost no one, {he/she} immediately approaches and breaks the ice without difficulty ({6-11: at the park, at a birthday party | 12-17: at a party, in a new group}).

After a disappointment ({6-11: a scolding, losing a game, a plan falling through | 12-17: an argument, a bad outcome, a canceled plan}), {he/she} gets over it quickly and returns to good spirits in no time.
When facing something new or a change of plans, {he/she} assumes it will go well and focuses more on the positives than on what could go wrong.
When something happens to {him/her} (good or bad), the first thing {he/she} does is tell someone ({6-11: you, a friend | 12-17: friends, family}) rather than keeping it to {himself/herself}.

MEL.template: After something that affects {him/her} deeply (a scolding, a disappointment, an argument), {he/she} stays quiet or withdrawn for a long time — {6-11: longer than other children {his/her} age | 12-17: sometimes for hours or even days}.
Comments or criticisms that {6-11: other children | 12-17: other kids {his/her} age} would let roll off their backs still affect {him/her} for quite a while, sometimes days.

If something {he/she} does doesn't turn out as {he/she} thinks it should ({6-11: a drawing, homework, a craft | 12-17: a project, a grade, something {he/she} has created}), {he/she} doesn't consider it good enough and has trouble letting it go and moving on to something else, even when it's already fine.
{He/She} prefers being with one or two close people rather than joining a large group or a gathering with lots of people.

FLE.template: In moments of tension ({6-11: an argument at home, an emergency, a dispute among children | 12-17: family arguments, friend drama, exams}), {he/she} stays calm even when others get upset.
When faced with something new (a group, an activity), {he/she} prefers to observe from the sidelines for a while before joining in, rather than jumping right in.
If {he/she} can get what {he/she} wants without arguing or fighting, {he/she} almost always chooses that path.
When a routine or way of doing things works for {him/her}, {he/she} feels no need to change it just to try something else; {he/she} is comfortable with the familiar.

COL.template: When {6-11: another child challenges or contradicts {him/her} | 12-17: someone confronts {him/her} (a peer, a sibling, an adult)}, {his/her} first impulse is to stand {his/her} ground, not to de-escalate.
{He/She} decides quickly and gets moving even when not everything is clear; {he/she} prefers acting over waiting.

SAN.template: New and unknown things attract and excite {him/her}, even if {he/she} doesn't yet know how they will turn out.
{He/She} starts {6-11: games or hobbies | 12-17: activities or interests} with great enthusiasm, but once the novelty wears off, {he/she} gets bored and wants to switch to something else.

MEL.template: When something truly affects {him/her}, {he/she} only tells one or two specific people {he/she} trusts.
Small details that {6-11: other children wouldn't even notice | 12-17: others wouldn't pay attention to} (a brusque tone, a glance, a passing comment) continue to affect {him/her} for a long while.

FLE.template: When others get rushed or nervous ({6-11: an emergency, a chaotic group | 12-17: emergencies, drama, peer pressure}), {he/she} maintains {his/her} own pace without getting caught up in it.
{He/She} prefers sticking with what {he/she} already knows rather than jumping into something new just for the sake of trying.

COL.template: When something goes well for {him/her} or makes {him/her} happy, the good mood lasts a long time, not just for the moment.
Worries rarely keep {him/her} from sleeping or eating.
When something goes wrong, {he/she} processes it quickly and moves on right away.
After making an important decision for {himself/herself}, {he/she} continues to mull it over even after it's already been made.
There are people or situations that continue to weigh on {him/her} even after they're over, even when {he/she} knows they shouldn't.
Before something important ({6-11: an exam, a performance, a birthday party | 12-17: an exam, a presentation, a social event}), tension or worry shows in advance.

SAN.template: When something outrages {him/her}, {he/she} has trouble hiding it even when {he/she} knows {he/she} should.
{His/Her} mood can swing from very high to very low (or vice versa) within the same day.
When something truly matters to {him/her}, {he/she} reacts with considerable intensity.
When there's tension in a group ({6-11: among siblings, among friends | 12-17: among friends, at home}), {he/she} is usually one of those who stays calm.
New situations don't usually make {him/her} nervous.
{His/Her} mood stays fairly steady throughout the day.

MEL.template: When {he/she} knows what {he/she} thinks, {he/she} says it even if it makes {him/her} unpopular.
{He/She} finds it hard to let an injustice pass without reacting (in class, with siblings, with friends).
If something matters to {him/her}, {he/she} has no trouble defending it in front of others.
Even when {he/she} has a very clear opinion, {he/she} feels no tension keeping it to {himself/herself}.
When {he/she} can avoid getting into an argument, {he/she} does.
{He/She} prefers to observe the situation first and take a position afterward.

FLE.template: {He/She} finds it easy to strike up a conversation with almost anyone.
{He/She} enjoys social gatherings (birthdays, get-togethers, meetups) even if they're brief or occasional.
With new groups or people, {he/she} has no trouble fitting in.
After a day with lots of people, {he/she} needs some time alone (in {his/her} room, without people around) to recharge.
At large gatherings (parties, family events), {he/she} prefers to stay in the background.
Tensions in the environment ({6-11: at home, at school | 12-17: at home, among friends, at school}) affect {him/her} more than is visible on the surface.
